Leviticus 6 confronts us with a truth we would rather avoid: sin against God always involves sin against others. The chapter opens not with abstract religious duty but with concrete betrayal—someone has lied about a deposit, stolen from a neighbor, extorted a vulnerable person, or lied about found property. What strikes us is that God calls this "acts unfaithfully against the LORD." When you deceive your neighbor for gain, you have not simply committed a social crime; you have sinned against God Himself. This means the secular view of morality—that dishonesty is merely a private transaction between two people—is fundamentally wrong. God's ownership of all things, and His sovereignty over all relationships, makes every act of deception a rebellion against His authority. The remedy requires both restitution and sacrifice. The guilty party must repay in full plus twenty percent, acknowledging that what they stole was never theirs to take. Only then, with restoration made and guilt offering brought, does the priest make atonement before the LORD. This is the order: acknowledgment, restitution, sacrifice, forgiveness. Not one step can be skipped.

The remainder of the chapter reveals the relentless devotion required of those who approach God. The altar fire must burn continually through every night, never extinguished. Each morning the priest must remove yesterday's ashes while maintaining the flame—a painstaking cycle of service without break or shortcuts. The grain offerings, the priestly grain offering that is burned entirely, the sin offerings handled with meticulous care—all of this speaks to a God who takes His worship seriously and expects those who serve Him to do the same. These are not suggestions or general guidelines. They are precise commands about where things happen, who touches them, what becomes holy, what must be burned versus eaten. When the text says "anything that touches them will become holy," it reveals a view of holiness that is contagious and transformative. You cannot approach God casually. Carelessness in worship is a form of disrespect.

What Leviticus 6 demands of us today is a reckoning: Are we willing to be people who restore what we have taken, who acknowledge our guilt before both God and those we have wronged? The specificity of these ancient laws—about oil and flour, about which pot to use, about when fire must burn—teaches us that true worship cannot exist where we are dishonest in our dealings or lazy in our devotion. We live under a New Covenant where Christ Himself is our guilt offering, our sin offering, our perpetual fire burning in our place. But that does not diminish the weight of this chapter. If anything, it deepens it. Christ paid the full price plus infinitely more. How then can we approach Him half-heartedly? How can we deceive our neighbor and imagine ourselves acceptable to Him? Leviticus 6 calls us to the kind of thoroughness, honesty, and reverence that befits a people who have been ransomed at such a cost.
